summary: In Book IV of the Nicomachean Ethics, Aristotle examines specific moral virtues, applying his doctrine of the mean to wealth, honor, anger, and social interaction. He details how virtues like liberality, magnificence, pride, good temper, and truthfulness represent the intermediate state between extremes of excess and deficiency.

principles.statement: Virtuous giving requires giving to the right people, in the right amounts, at the right time, and for the sake of the noble.
principles.rationale: Liberality is not merely the act of giving away money, but giving it correctly and purposefully without depleting one's ability to help the right causes.
principles.source_quote: Therefore the liberal man, like other virtuous men, will give for the sake of the noble, and rightly; for he will give to the right people, the right amounts, and at the right time...

principles.statement: True pride requires perfect virtue and goodness of character.
principles.rationale: A proud man claims great things because he actually deserves them. Claiming great things without virtue is merely vanity and insolence.
principles.source_quote: Pride, then, seems to be a sort of crown of the virtues; for it makes them greater, and it is not found without them. Therefore it is hard to be truly proud; for it is impossible without nobility and goodness of character.

principles.statement: Truthfulness is inherently noble, while falsehood is inherently base.
principles.rationale: A virtuous person loves truth for its own sake and avoids falsehood even when nothing is at stake.
principles.source_quote: And falsehood is in itself mean and culpable, and truth noble and worthy of praise.

mental_models.name: The Doctrine of the Mean
mental_models.description: A framework for understanding virtue as the intermediate state between two vices: one of excess and one of deficiency.
mental_models.example: Liberality is the mean with regard to wealth, sitting between the excess of prodigality (wasting substance) and the deficiency of meanness (caring too much for wealth).

mental_models.name: Relativity of Scale
mental_models.description: The appropriateness of an action or expenditure depends on the agent's means, the circumstances, and the object, rather than an absolute amount.
mental_models.example: The expense of equipping a trireme is not the same as that of heading a sacred embassy. It is what is fitting, then, in relation to the agent, and to the circumstances and the object.

heuristics: It is more characteristic of virtue to do good than to have good done to one.
When in doubt about claims of oneself, lean toward understatement rather than exaggeration, as it is in better taste.
Be open in your hate and in your love; concealing feelings is cowardly.

signature_quotes.text: For it is more characteristic of virtue to do good than to have good done to one, and more characteristic to do what is noble than not to do what is base...
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why the liberal man is praised more for giving than for taking.

signature_quotes.text: Pride, then, seems to be a sort of crown of the virtues; for it makes them greater, and it is not found without them.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the relationship between pride (magnanimity) and the other virtues.

signature_quotes.text: And falsehood is in itself mean and culpable, and truth noble and worthy of praise.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the virtue of truthfulness versus boastfulness and mock-modesty.

signature_quotes.text: For the sense of disgrace is not even characteristic of a good man, since it is consequent on bad actions...
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why shame is not a virtue, but merely a feeling appropriate only for the young.

anti_patterns.description: Giving money to anyone and everyone recklessly.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It exhausts your resources, leaving you unable to give to the right people at the right time, and often forces you to take from wrong sources to fund your spending.

anti_patterns.description: Concealing one's true feelings or opinions to appease others.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a coward's part and shows a lack of self-respect. A truly proud and virtuous person cares more for truth than for what people will think.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that feeling shame makes an adult a good person.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Shame is a reaction to doing something disgraceful. A truly good person does not voluntarily commit bad actions, so they should have no reason to feel shame.
